items.html:
The Health and Safety at Work Act 1974

The Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 is the main piece of health and safety law in England. It places a duty on your employer to protect the health, safety and welfare of everyone affected by their work — you, the people you support, visitors and members of the public.

Your employer must do this ‘so far as is reasonably practicable’. In plain terms, they must weigh the size of a risk against the time, trouble and cost of controlling it, and take sensible steps rather than ignore hazards or spend money on dangers that are trivial.

Your duties and the 1999 Regulations

Health and safety is a shared responsibility, so the Act also gives you legal duties. The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 build on this and require your employer to carry out risk assessments and give you information and training. Your part of the bargain is straightforward:

  • Take reasonable care: look after your own safety and that of others affected by what you do.
  • Co-operate: follow safe systems of work and your employer’s policies.
  • Use things properly: use equipment and substances the way you were trained to.
  • Report problems: tell your employer about hazards or gaps in protection.

items.question: Under health and safety law, which of these is a duty that falls on YOU as an employee rather than on your employer?

answers.text: Taking reasonable care of your own safety and co-operating with safe systems of work.
answers.feedback: Correct. The Act gives employees a duty to take reasonable care and to co-operate with the arrangements their employer puts in place.

answers.text: Carrying out the workplace risk assessments.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Arranging risk assessments is the employer’s duty under the 1999 Regulations, though you help by reporting hazards.

answers.text: Paying for the protective equipment you need.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Your employer must provide necessary protective equipment free of charge; the cost is not yours to carry.

answers.text: Writing the organisation’s health and safety policy.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Producing the policy is an employer responsibility, not something expected of a care worker.

items.html:
Hazardous substances and COSHH

Care work involves more hazardous substances than you might expect — cleaning products, disinfectants, thickening powders, some creams, and clinical waste can all cause harm if used carelessly. The Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations, known as COSHH, require your employer to assess these substances and tell you how to use them safely.

Harm can come from swallowing, breathing in fumes, splashes to the eyes or skin, or contact with broken skin. Many everyday products are safe only because people follow the instructions on them.

Labels, storage and spills

The label and the safety data sheet tell you what a substance can do and how to work with it. Orange or red-and-white diamond hazard symbols warn of dangers such as corrosive, harmful or flammable contents.

  • Read before you use: follow the label and any COSHH information for that product.
  • Never decant or mix: keep substances in their original labelled containers and never combine cleaning chemicals.
  • Store safely: keep products locked away from the people you support, especially where anyone may be confused.
  • Deal with spills: follow your training, use the right protective equipment, and report the spill.

items.question: To save space, it is acceptable to pour a cleaning product into an unlabelled bottle as long as you remember what is in it.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. Substances should stay in their original labelled containers so that everyone can see the hazards and first-aid advice, and so no one is poisoned by mistake.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. This is false: substances must stay in their original labelled containers so the hazard warnings and safety advice are always visible to everyone.

items.html:
Preventing fire

Fire needs three things together — a source of heat, fuel to burn and oxygen from the air. Take any one away and a fire cannot start or continue. The Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 makes your employer responsible for assessing fire risks and keeping people safe, but prevention is part of everyone’s job.

You help by keeping fire exits and corridors clear, reporting damaged sockets or overloaded plugs, being alert around cooking, smoking materials and heaters, and knowing where alarms and extinguishers are.

If you discover a fire

In a fire, seconds matter and lives come before property. Learn your workplace fire procedure before you ever need it.

  • Raise the alarm: sound the nearest fire alarm and warn people nearby.
  • Get out and stay out: help people evacuate by the nearest safe route and do not go back in.
  • Call 999: make sure the fire service has been called.
  • Do not take risks: only ever tackle a tiny fire if you are trained and can do so safely.

People who cannot escape unaided should have a Personal Emergency Evacuation Plan (PEEP) that sets out the help they need.

items.question: You discover a small fire has taken hold in a waste bin and is spreading to a curtain. What should you do first?

answers.text: Raise the alarm, help people evacuate and make sure 999 is called.
answers.feedback: Correct. Warning people and getting them out safely comes first; property can be replaced but people cannot.

answers.text: Look for an extinguisher and try to put it out yourself.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A fire already spreading to a curtain is beyond a small contained fire; raising the alarm and evacuating must come first.

answers.text: Open the windows to let the smoke out.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Extra air feeds a fire, and the priority is to warn people and evacuate, not to ventilate the room.

answers.text: Go and find your manager to ask what to do.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. There is no time to search for a manager; follow the fire procedure by raising the alarm straight away.

items.html:
Work equipment: PUWER and LOLER

Hoists, slings, wheelchairs, beds and pressure-relieving mattresses all count as work equipment. The Provision and Use of Work Equipment Regulations (PUWER) require it to be suitable, maintained and used by people who are trained. Where equipment lifts a person, the Lifting Operations and Lifting Equipment Regulations (LOLER) add regular thorough examinations, usually every six months for a hoist and its sling.

Only ever use equipment you have been trained on, and check it looks sound and within its inspection date before you rely on it.

Faulty equipment, gas and electrical safety

Never use equipment that seems damaged or is out of test date, even if you are busy. Take it out of use, label it clearly and report it so it can be repaired or replaced.

  • Faulty electrics: report frayed cables, cracked plugs or scorch marks and do not use the item.
  • Gas smells: if you smell gas in someone’s home, avoid using switches or naked flames, get fresh air in and follow your procedure.
  • Carbon monoxide: take a sounding alarm seriously and act on it.
  • Security: follow key-safe and door procedures, and confirm the identity of callers.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about equipment and safety.
items.textField: Lifting equipment such as a hoist is thoroughly examined under the *LOLER* regulations, while work equipment in general is covered by *PUWER*. If a piece of equipment looks faulty, you must take it out of *use* and report it. If you smell gas in a home, you should not touch any electrical *switch*. You should only ever operate equipment you have been *trained* to use.

items.html:
Accidents, sudden illness and RIDDOR

If someone has an accident or becomes suddenly unwell, stay calm and keep yourself safe first. Give only the first aid you are trained and authorised to give, call 999 for anything serious such as chest pain, difficulty breathing or a suspected stroke, and stay with the person until help arrives.

Afterwards, record what happened and report it. Certain serious events must be reported to the Health and Safety Executive under RIDDOR — the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations 2013. Your manager usually makes the RIDDOR report; your job is to raise the alarm and write an accurate account.

Looking after your own wellbeing

Care work is rewarding but it can be physically and emotionally demanding. Your own health and safety matters too, and managing stress is part of Care Certificate Standard 13.

  • Know the signs: poor sleep, irritability, exhaustion and dread can all point to stress.
  • Talk about it: raise how you feel with your manager or a trusted colleague early.
  • Use support: supervision, employee assistance schemes and your GP are all there to help.
  • Protect your limits: take your breaks and speak up if workloads are unsafe.

items.question: You arrive for a visit and find Mrs Okafor on the floor; she is conscious but has hurt her hip and cannot get up. What is the best course of action?

answers.text: Keep her comfortable and reassured, call 999, avoid moving her, then report and record the incident.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reassurance, avoiding a risky move, calling for medical help and then reporting is safe practice; the fall may also need a RIDDOR report by your manager.

answers.text: Use the hoist on your own to lift her back into her chair quickly.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Lifting someone with a possible hip injury, and hoisting alone, could cause serious harm and is beyond safe practice.

answers.text: Help her stand by pulling her up under the arms so she is not embarrassed.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Pulling her up risks worsening the injury and injuring your own back; she should not be moved before help arrives.

answers.text: Wait until the end of your shift to mention it, as she is talking and seems alright.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A fall with injury needs prompt medical attention and prompt reporting, not a delay until later.
